title: GDPR Compliance (Basic)
description: Basic GDPR (EU General Data Protection Regulation) compliance pattern. Implements data protection principles: lawful processing, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, and security. Includes consent management, privacy notices, data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure), breach notification, and DPO appointment where required.

provides.reasoning: This is the core capability of the pattern - providing GDPR compliance framework including data protection principles, consent management, and data subject rights implementation.
Consent management is a fundamental GDPR requirement and a distinct capability that tracks, manages, and enforces user consent for data processing activities.
Implements GDPR data subject rights (right to access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction) which is a specific, well-defined capability required by the regulation.
GDPR requires transparent privacy notices which is a distinct capability from consent management, ensuring users are informed about data processing activities.
The pattern implements breach notification processes as required by GDPR Article 33-34 for reporting breaches to authorities and affected individuals.

requires.description: Audit logs for GDPR compliance verification
requires.reasoning: GDPR Article 30 requires maintaining records of processing activities, and demonstrating compliance requires comprehensive audit trails of all data operations, consent changes, and rights requests.
GDPR Article 32 requires appropriate security measures including encryption of personal data to ensure confidentiality and protect against unauthorized access.
Storage limitation principle requires automated data retention and deletion policies to ensure personal data is not kept longer than necessary for specified purposes.
GDPR compliance requires knowing what personal data exists and where it's stored to fulfill subject rights requests and maintain records of processing activities.
GDPR requires restricting access to personal data to authorized personnel only through proper access controls to maintain data confidentiality and integrity.

retention_days.description: Data retention period in days. GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation - data kept only as long as necessary for processing purposes. Shorter is better for compliance.

encryption_at_rest.description: Encrypt personal data at rest. GDPR Article 32 requires appropriate technical measures including encryption. Recommended for all personal data.

encryption_in_transit.description: Encrypt personal data in transit using TLS/SSL. GDPR Article 32 security requirement. Essential for protecting data during transmission.

dpo_required.description: Data Protection Officer appointment required. GDPR Article 37 mandates DPO for public authorities, large-scale monitoring, or large-scale special category data processing.

consent_granularity.description: Consent management granularity. GDPR Article 7 requires specific, informed consent. Purpose-specific is best practice (separate consent per processing purpose), per-data-category groups by data type, global is least compliant.

breach_notification_hours.description: Hours to notify supervisory authority of data breach. GDPR Article 33 requires notification within 72 hours unless unlikely to result in risk. Lower values ensure faster compliance.

data_subject_request_days.description: Days to respond to data subject access requests (DSAR). GDPR Article 12 requires response within one month. Shorter response times improve compliance posture.

pseudonymization_enabled.description: Enable pseudonymization of personal data. GDPR Article 25 and 32 recommend pseudonymization as data protection by design measure. Reduces risk if data is breached.

cross_border_transfers.description: Mechanism for transferring personal data outside EEA. GDPR Chapter V requirements: eea-only (no transfers), adequacy-decision (approved countries), standard-contractual-clauses (most common), binding-corporate-rules (for multinationals).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: GDPR compliance requires ongoing operational costs including: consent management platform ($50-$2000/month for basic to enterprise SaaS solutions like OneTrust Starter vs Enterprise), data subject request management tools ($50-$1500/month), privacy notice management systems ($20-$500/month), audit logging and monitoring infrastructure ($30-$1000/month for log retention and analysis), encrypted data storage overhead (10-20% premium on standard storage, $20-$500/month), data breach detection and monitoring tools ($30-$2000/month), and DPO services if outsourced ($0-$1500/month for part-time or fractional DPO). Minimum represents small business with basic tooling and minimal data processing; maximum represents mid-to-large enterprise with comprehensive GDPR tooling, extensive data processing, and professional DPO services.
provenance.adoptionCost: GDPR compliance implementation is highly complex and labor-intensive. Requires: comprehensive data mapping and inventory across all systems (40-80 hours), privacy impact assessments (PIA/DPIA) for high-risk processing (20-40 hours), implementing technical controls including consent management, data encryption, access controls, and audit logging (80-120 hours), developing and documenting policies, procedures, and privacy notices (40-60 hours), configuring data subject rights workflows (access, rectification, erasure, portability) (60-100 hours), setting up breach notification procedures and systems (20-40 hours), staff training on GDPR principles and procedures (20-40 hours), and legal review of all documentation and processes (20-40 hours). Even with AI coding assistants helping with technical implementation, significant legal, compliance, and process design expertise is required. Estimated 300-480 total hours at blended rates of $50-$150/hour for mix of legal, compliance, and technical resources.
provenance.licenseCost: Basic GDPR compliance can leverage primarily open-source tools with some commercial components. Core open-source solutions available for consent management (e.g., Klaro), data encryption libraries, and audit logging. However, most organizations invest in commercial privacy management platforms for efficiency and reduced liability. Entry-level commercial consent management platforms start at $2000-$5000 one-time setup fees (plus monthly SaaS costs). Additional one-time costs may include privacy policy generators/templates ($500-$1500), GDPR compliance assessment tools ($500-$2000), and specialized data discovery/mapping tools ($1000-$3000 setup). Organizations with minimal requirements using primarily open-source tools may have near-zero license costs, while those adopting commercial platforms will incur $5000-$15000 in initial setup and license fees.

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern is only selected when GDPR compliance is explicitly required as primary regulatory obligation.

requires_nfr.reason: GDPR compliance processes (consent management, data subject rights workflows) support up to four nines availability.
GDPR workflow overhead (consent checks, audit logging, privacy controls) requires at least 100ms p95 latency.
Data subject rights request processing and breach notification workflows require at least 500ms p99 latency.
GDPR compliance infrastructure (consent systems, privacy controls, DPO coordination) requires at least 5 minutes RTO.
GDPR Article 30 requires records of processing activities; audit logging is mandatory for compliance.
GDPR exclusively regulates personal data (PII); pattern only applies when PII is present.

supports_constraints.reason: GDPR compliance is cloud-agnostic; regulations apply regardless of infrastructure choice.
GDPR compliance is a legal/regulatory pattern that applies to any application handling personal data regardless of language.
GDPR compliance requirements apply to any platform that processes personal data.
